factors.summary: The company is in growth mode, increasing headcount to support operational expansion.

evidence.text: Average full-time equivalent employees increased by 25.9%, reflecting a ramp-up in operations post-pandemic.

factors.summary: Labor relations are stable with recent agreements improving terms for employees.

evidence.text: In April 2022, Alaska's dispatchers ratified an agreement that includes increased pay and enhanced benefits.

factors.summary: New initiatives include the Ascend Pilot Academy, enhancing diversity in aviation.

evidence.text: We launched the Ascend Pilot Academy, which will provide aspiring pilots a simpler and more financially accessible path to become a pilot at Horizon.

factors.summary: ESG commitments are integrated into company strategy, including diversity goals and carbon emissions targets.

evidence.text: Delivering on our diversity, equity and inclusion goals is critical to our long-term success.

factors.summary: The company is differentiating through operational efficiency and strategic fleet transitions.

evidence.text: Moving to single fleet at Alaska and Horizon strengthens our low cost, high productivity competitive advantage.

factors.summary: Order backlog is growing, particularly with new aircraft deliveries.

evidence.text: As of March 31, 2022, Alaska has firm orders to purchase 67 Boeing 737 aircraft with deliveries in 2022 through 2024 and firm commitments to lease seven Boeing 737-9 aircraft with deliveries in 2022.

factors.summary: Supply chain health is stable, with aircraft deliveries as planned.

evidence.text: As of March 31, 2022, Alaska has firm orders to purchase 67 Boeing 737 aircraft with deliveries in 2022 through 2024 and firm commitments to lease seven Boeing 737-9 aircraft with deliveries in 2022.

factors.summary: Inventory levels are stable, with no significant risks of obsolescence or shortage.

evidence.text: The portfolio is managed by reputable firms that adhere to our investment policy that sets forth investment objectives, approved and prohibited investments, and duration and credit quality guidelines.

factors.summary: Product quality and safety are maintained, with no significant incidents reported.

evidence.text: No significant quality or safety incidents were reported in the first quarter of 2022.

factors.summary: Route network changes are expected to be accretive, focusing on profitable routes.

evidence.text: Horizon has commitments to purchase 12 Embraer E175 aircraft with deliveries between 2022 and 2025. Alaska has options to acquire up to 11 additional Boeing 737-9 aircraft and 41 additional Boeing 737-10 aircraft with deliveries between 2024 and 2026.

factors.summary: Debt leverage is stable, with the debt-to-capitalization ratio within target range.

evidence.text: During the three months ended March 31, 2022, we took free and clear delivery of seven Boeing 737-9 aircraft. [...] We ended the quarter with a debt-to-capitalization ratio of 50%, within our stated target range of 40% to 50%.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are focused on debt reduction and liquidity maintenance, indicating management confidence.

evidence.text: As the business returns to sustained profitability, reducing outstanding debt, normalizing our on-hand liquidity, and reinforcing our balance sheet remain high priorities.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is improving, supporting liquidity and growth initiatives.

evidence.text: Net cash provided by operating activities was $287 million, compared to $167 million during the same period in 2021. [...] The increase was primarily attributable to increased build in our air traffic liability.

factors.summary: M&A activity is focused on strategic capacity expansion through agreements with Horizon and SkyWest.

evidence.text: Alaska also has an agreement with SkyWest Airlines to expand their long-term capacity purchase agreement by six Embraer E175 aircraft in 2022 and one in 2023.

factors.summary: Regulatory changes are not significantly impacting the company, with no major new costs or restrictions mentioned.

evidence.text: No significant regulatory changes impacting the company were reported in the first quarter of 2022.

factors.summary: Trade tariffs are not significantly affecting the company's operations or costs.

evidence.text: No significant impact from trade tariffs was reported in the first quarter of 2022.

factors.summary: Litigation exposure is minimal, with no material cases affecting the company.

evidence.text: The company is not currently facing material litigation or investigations that could result in significant financial or operational impact.

factors.summary: Economic conditions are favorable, supporting demand for air travel.

evidence.text: The increase in demand for air travel is driving higher passenger revenues and operational improvements.

factors.summary: Interest rate changes are manageable, with the company maintaining a stable debt structure.

evidence.text: We ended the quarter with a debt-to-capitalization ratio of 50%, within our stated target range of 40% to 50%.

factors.summary: Foreign exchange exposure is minimal, with no significant impact on translation or margins.

evidence.text: The company's operations are primarily focused in the US, with limited foreign exchange exposure.

factors.summary: Geopolitical risks are not material, with no significant disruptions or opportunities reported.

evidence.text: No significant geopolitical risks impacting the company were reported in the first quarter of 2022.

factors.summary: The company demonstrates strong pricing power, with consistent increases in ticket yields across multiple regions, contributing to higher passenger revenues.

evidence.text: Ticket yields increased by 17% year-over-year, contributing to higher passenger revenue.
Regional passenger revenues increased significantly compared to the first quarter of 2021, primarily driven by increased load factors and a 31% improvement in yield.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is accelerating across multiple segments, driven by increased demand and operational improvements.

evidence.text: We expect to see continued growth to Passenger revenue as we progress through 2022 driven by high demand and increased capacity.
The $270 million improvement was primarily driven by a $737 million increase in Passenger revenues as a result of increased demand for air travel.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting positively towards higher-margin segments, particularly in cargo and regional operations. This shift is supported by increases in cargo and other revenue, as well as regional passenger revenues, though there are associated cost increases such as fuel costs.

evidence.text: Cargo and other revenue increased by 32%, driven by dedicated freighters and belly cargo activity.
Regional passenger revenues increased significantly compared to the first quarter of 2021, [...] partially offset by a $21 million increase in fuel costs.

factors.summary: Demand trends are positive, with a rebound in March 2022 following the Omicron wave, and an overall acceleration supported by increased departures and passenger numbers.

evidence.text: As cases subsided and business and leisure demand rebounded, monthly operating revenues surpassed 2019 levels in the month of March, a first since the pandemic began.
evidence.subsection: Business Recovery and First Quarter Results

evidence.text: Landing fees and other rentals increased by $9 million, or 7%, during the first quarter of 2022. [...] This increase is in line with the 86% increase in revenue passengers as compared to the prior-year period.

factors.summary: Volume trends are positive, with significant growth in both revenue passengers and RPMs, as well as an increase in contracted services driven by higher departures and passenger numbers.

evidence.text: Revenue passengers increased by 86.3% year-over-year, and RPMs increased by 96.3%.

Contracted services increased by $27 million, or 53%, during the first quarter of 2022 compared to the same period in 2021 driven primarily by increased departures and passengers in line with increased demand, [...]

factors.summary: The end-market momentum is positive, with both leisure and business travel showing recovery. Regional operations have particularly strong growth, evidenced by improved financial results.

evidence.text: Surging demand in March by both business and leisure travelers drove meaningful improvements to year-over-year results.

Regional operations recorded an adjusted pretax loss of $55 million in the first quarter of 2022, compared to an adjusted pretax loss of $150 million in the first quarter of 2021. Improved results were attributable to a $113 million increase in operating revenues, [...]

factors.summary: Input costs, particularly fuel, are rising significantly, creating margin pressure across the company. Both fuel expense and non-fuel operating expenses have increased, driven by higher fuel prices and increased consumption.

evidence.text: Fuel expense increased by 71%, driven by higher fuel prices and increased consumption.
Higher economic fuel prices, combined with more gallons consumed, drove the increase in Mainline fuel expense.
Non-fuel operating expenses increased significantly, driven by increased variable costs, largely consistent with the overall increase in capacity and departures.

factors.summary: Labor costs are rising due to higher wages, benefits, and increased FTEs, which have led to significant increases in expenses.

evidence.text: Wages and benefits increased by 23%, primarily due to a 26% increase in FTEs.

Other expense increased $47 million, or 45%, during the first quarter of 2022 compared to the same period in 2021. Increased expense is primarily driven by incremental crew hotel stays and per diem, consistent with the overall increase in departures and capacity.

factors.summary: Cost reduction programs are in place and showing measurable savings, which support margin improvement. However, these savings may be offset by rising costs, indicating a mixed outcome.

evidence.text: We believe it is useful to summarize operating expenses as follows, which is consistent with the way expenses are reported internally and evaluated by management.
Adjusted net debt to EBITDAR improved from 2.7x to 1.6x, reflecting better financial health and cost management.

factors.summary: Productivity is improving across the company, as evidenced by increases in load factors and RASM. Both regional and operational statistics reflect positive trends, with significant improvements in passenger revenues and yields contributing to overall productivity gains.

evidence.text: Load factor improved from 51.9% to 76.8%, and RASM increased by 59.1% year-over-year.
Regional passenger revenues increased significantly compared to the first quarter of 2021, primarily driven by increased load factors and a 31% improvement in yield.

factors.summary: The margin trajectory shows mixed performance, with non-fuel margins improving but overall operating margins under pressure due to rising costs.

evidence.text: CASM excluding fuel and special items decreased slightly, but total operating expenses increased by 97% year-over-year.

Mainline operations recorded an adjusted pretax loss of $174 million in the first quarter of 2022, compared to an adjusted pretax loss of $444 million in the first quarter of 2021. [...] The $270 million improvement was primarily driven by a $737 million increase in Passenger revenues as a result of increased demand for air travel, offset by a $301 million increase in non-fuel operating costs and a $207 million increase in economic fuel cost.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is increasing, driven by higher demand, operational ramp-up, higher departures, and load factors. The increase is partially offset by decreases in rates at some airports, but overall, the trend is positive.

evidence.text: Aircraft utilization increased by 11.8%, reflecting higher capacity and demand.

Landing fees and other rentals increased by $9 million, or 7%, during the first quarter of 2022. The increase compared to the same period in 2021 is due to an increase in departures as demand returns, partially offset by decreases in rates at some of the airports we service.

factors.summary: Management exhibits mixed confidence, with cautious optimism in strategic investments and a strong focus on financial stability.

evidence.text: We are focused on staffing our airlines and delivering the operational excellence for which we are known.
As the business returns to sustained profitability, reducing outstanding debt, normalizing our on-hand liquidity, and reinforcing our balance sheet remain high priorities.

factors.summary: Management maintains a cautious outlook due to rising fuel costs and labor challenges, yet expresses confidence in future performance supported by sufficient liquidity.

evidence.text: We expect to see continued pressure in aircraft fuel expense as we progress through 2022, driven by both increased raw fuel and refining margins on increased capacity.

We believe that our current cash and marketable securities balance, combined with available sources of liquidity, will be sufficient to fund our operations, meet our debt payment obligations, and remain in compliance with the financial debt covenants in existing financing arrangements for the foreseeable future.

factors.summary: Near-term risks include labor shortages, fuel price volatility, rising fuel costs, potential Omicron variant impacts, and economic downturns. These risks could affect operations and financial performance in the short term.

evidence.text: We faced challenges in the first quarter as the omicron variant caused disruptions in our pilot training throughput, which led to a shortage of pilots as we entered the second quarter.
Higher economic fuel prices, combined with a significant increase in gallons consumed, drove the increase in Regional fuel expense.

factors.summary: The company has a clear long-term strategy focusing on fleet modernization, cost efficiency, and ESG initiatives, including diversity goals and carbon emissions targets. Additionally, there is a strategic direction towards capacity expansion through aircraft purchases and leases.

evidence.text: We have tied a portion of long-term executive compensation to achievement of diversity goals. Additionally, we have incorporated a carbon emissions target into our company-wide Performance-Based Pay Plan.
Alaska has firm orders to purchase 67 Boeing 737 aircraft with deliveries in 2022 through 2024 and firm commitments to lease seven Boeing 737-9 aircraft with deliveries in 2022.

factors.summary: Fuel costs and economic conditions are key sensitivities impacting near-term results.

evidence.text: Although we are presenting these non-GAAP amounts for the reasons above, investors and other readers should not necessarily conclude that these amounts are non-recurring, infrequent, or unusual in nature.
Higher economic fuel prices, combined with more gallons consumed, drove the increase in Mainline fuel expense.

factors.summary: New co-branded credit card agreements and fleet transitions are expected to drive growth. The company is also expanding its capacity with new aircraft deliveries.

evidence.text: In the first quarter, we announced a renewed co-brand credit card agreement with Bank of America extending to 2030.
We expect third-party regional carrier expense to grow in 2022 as compared to 2021 as we bring incremental E175 aircraft into the CPA with SkyWest through the year.

factors.summary: The company is making strides in environmental compliance, as evidenced by its incorporation of a carbon emissions target into its strategy and its investment in more fuel-efficient aircraft. These actions demonstrate a commitment to sustainability and efficiency.

evidence.text: We have incorporated a carbon emissions target into our company-wide Performance-Based Pay Plan, which is currently tracking to target achievement.
The company is positioning itself for efficiency gains with new aircraft orders, which are more fuel-efficient.

factors.summary: The travel demand mix is shifting favorably towards both leisure and business travel, which is aiding in the recovery and improving financial performance. This shift is characterized by increased demand and higher yields due to favorable travel patterns.

evidence.text: Surging demand in March by both business and leisure travelers drove meaningful improvements to year-over-year results.
The increase in yield is a result of higher demand for air travel and increased load factors.

factors.summary: The company is facing rising fuel costs and expects continued pressure in 2022. However, their hedging strategy, which includes using call options to cap crude oil costs, appears favorable with set prices for the second, third, and fourth quarters of 2022 at $71, $80, and $83 per barrel respectively.

evidence.text: We expect to see continued pressure in aircraft fuel expense as we progress through 2022, driven by both increased raw fuel and refining margins on increased capacity.

All of our future oil positions are call options, which are designed to effectively cap the cost of the crude oil component of our jet fuel purchases. [...] Weighted-Average Crude Oil Price per Barrel: Second Quarter 2022 - $71, Third Quarter 2022 - $80, Fourth Quarter 2022 - $83.

factors.summary: The factor shows positive trends with increasing capacity, improving load factors, and rising revenue metrics. However, there is a mixed performance in Mainline operations, despite improvements in passenger revenues.

evidence.text: Load factor improved from 51.9% to 76.8%, and RASM increased by 59.1% year-over-year.

Mainline operations recorded an adjusted pretax loss of $174 million in the first quarter of 2022, compared to an adjusted pretax loss of $444 million in the first quarter of 2021. [...] The $270 million improvement was primarily driven by a $737 million increase in Passenger revenues as a result of increased demand for air travel, offset by a $301 million increase in non-fuel operating costs and a $207 million increase in economic fuel cost.

factors.summary: The company is actively transitioning to a single fleet across its operations, which is expected to enhance efficiency. This strategy includes retiring the Q400 fleet by the end of 2023 and making significant investments in new aircraft through firm orders and lease commitments for Boeing 737 models.

evidence.text: We announced plans to accelerate our transition to a single fleet at Alaska, and revealed new plans to transition our regional operations to a single fleet by retiring our Q400 fleet by the end of 2023.
Alaska has firm orders to purchase 67 Boeing 737 aircraft with deliveries in 2022 through 2024 and firm commitments to lease seven Boeing 737-9 aircraft with deliveries in 2022.

factors.summary: The company anticipates an increase in capital expenditures, reflecting confidence in future growth through fleet modernization. The expected CapEx range of $1.6 billion to $1.7 billion for 2022 indicates a strategic focus on efficient growth and modernization.

evidence.text: Capital expenditures are expected to increase as the company invests in fleet modernization.
As part of the transition, we are also strategically upgauging our fleet as we backfill retired aircraft, supporting efficient growth.
Our capital expenditures for 2022 are expected to be approximately $1.6 billion to $1.7 billion, which we plan to fund with cash generated by operating activities and cash on hand.
